messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: So far, I'm finding the 1W is much more susceptible to background noise. I'm going to order a different solar management board and see if that helps my situation. I deployed my 1W to a site on Friday where I had been getting good service from a Rak4631 node, and it performed horribly. To the point of being unusable and unreachable. Literally swapped out the boards, and added the power pins for the 5v input to the header pins on my power board, and no other changes. Same battery, same solar board, same antenna. Ended up going back at the end of the day to put the 4631 back in the so I would have connectivity during the ice storm. The .250W board went right back to working great. Getting 16.5 mile connectivity tall building to tower range. Couldn't see the tower at all with the 1W board. I'm underimpressed so far. (edited)

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: Figured it out (I think). Commit 4f46ec7 removed NRF52BoardOTA and incorporated into into the base NRF52Board. The RAK3401Board.h header still calls for NRF52BoardOTA. It looks like recrof has a PR in line for merging to fix it here: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1507/changes/d5a73b239437ed6a1c9f9512246169e8238b25c8
messages.content_html: Figured it out (I think). Commit 4f46ec7 removed NRF52BoardOTA and incorporated into into the base NRF52Board. The RAK3401Board.h header still calls for NRF52BoardOTA. It looks like recrof has a PR in line for merging to fix it here: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1507/changes/d5a73b239437ed6a1c9f9512246169e8238b25c8
embeds_html:
fix: build errors because of changes in RAK3401Board base class by ...
+cleanup

messages.author: Fokai
messages.text: I only had the rak 1w up at the very first iteration of firmware and it fell behind the Heltec V4 with a ceramic filter by about 20% of packets received at my specific location. I now run a V4 with LNA bypass no filter and I'm very happy. I'd like to compare it again to the Rak 1w, but haven't found the time. V4 is darn good for the price and performance, but maybe the rak is better long term as the firmware gets better?

messages.author: paul / BostonMesh
messages.text: Are others encountering RX issues with the 3401/1W booster? I was running a stationG2 and 1W booster in my yard and disabled the repeat function on the G2. I’m also running meshcore-bot and noticed that the bot is missing commands sent it that are being heard by a nearby companion. In all cases, the 1W booster did not repeat the packet. I’m also seeing cases where the bot responds to a command but the packet was never repeated back out. I’m also running an observer and seeing that the bot successfully TX’d but the booster did not repeat it back out the mesh at large.

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: Is anyone getting stable production-ready results on the 3401? I had one working (or so I thought) at my house up in a tree. Took the box, mounted it on a frame, and took it to the site. I'm getting substantially worse performance from it than I was the 4631 test node I had at the site before. messages.author: andyshinn
messages.text: it definitely has more options for powering and other variables. for troubleshooting i think it is helpful to know the radio power path, antenna pigtail, antenna, etc. just based on previous experience alone on the higher power radios i would estimate that the 13302 is slightly more deaf than the 4631 (which is the "gold standard" at receiving in my experience). so, more messages may be getting to their destination. but the return ack may be more unreliable i've got mine now running for more than 48 hours on the ground after swapping out to a waveshare solar board since it claims 3A on the 5V output where most others only seem to be 1A. the RAK docs call out needing 1.5A on EX_5V. so, i imagine there are going to be power concerns that people run into a bunch
messages.content_html: it definitely has more options for powering and other variables. for troubleshooting i think it is helpful to know the radio power path, antenna pigtail, antenna, etc. just based on previous experience alone on the higher power radios i would estimate that the 13302 is slightly more deaf than the 4631 (which is the "gold standard" at receiving in my experience). so, more messages may be getting to their destination. but the return ack may be more unreliable i've got mine now running for more than 48 hours on the ground after swapping out to a waveshare solar board since it claims 3A on the 5V output where most others only seem to be 1A. the RAK docs call out needing 1.5A on EX_5V. so, i imagine there are going to be power concerns that people run into a bunch

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: Sure. I initially was using the DFRobot 5v Solar manager boards, but couldn't get stable results. Further research after I bought several of them indicate they have a reputation for being noisy. So, I ordered a Waveshare Solar Power Manager (D) board, hooked up to the RAK3401 via USB-C and out of the 5v green port to the 5v-EXT, with the jumper set to external power. It worked great at home. At home, it had a ALFA 915 5dbi antenna directly mounted on the bulkhead adapter on the top of the box. When I deployed it downtown, nothing changed on the inside of the box, other than hooking up the 12v solar panel to the waveshare board. Externally, I went with a Rokland 5.8dbi antenna (which was on my 4631 test node downtown), connected via a 3' piece of premade RFC-400 low loss with appropriate connectors which I also got from Rokland. I'm waiting on a second Waveshare board to be delivered to my house so I can put it on the other 1W that's there so I can eliminate that problem from the analysis loop. (edited)
messages.content_html: Sure. I initially was using the DFRobot 5v Solar manager boards, but couldn't get stable results. Further research after I bought several of them indicate they have a reputation for being noisy. So, I ordered a Waveshare Solar Power Manager (D) board, hooked up to the RAK3401 via USB-C and out of the 5v green port to the 5v-EXT, with the jumper set to external power. It worked great at home. At home, it had a ALFA 915 5dbi antenna directly mounted on the bulkhead adapter on the top of the box. When I deployed it downtown, nothing changed on the inside of the box, other than hooking up the 12v solar panel to the waveshare board. Externally, I went with a Rokland 5.8dbi antenna (which was on my 4631 test node downtown), connected via a 3' piece of premade RFC-400 low loss with appropriate connectors which I also got from Rokland. I'm waiting on a second Waveshare board to be delivered to my house so I can put it on the other 1W that's there so I can eliminate that problem from the analysis loop. (edited)

messages.author: andyshinn
messages.text: the dfrobot board is also only 1A output max, correct? it's possible that it spikes higher. but i also wonder if in that case it's dropping voltage a lot and the PA isn't getting what it needs. on the voltaic MPPT board i had it was rated for 1A but it was dropping enough that the MCU eventually didn't recover from a brownout the waveshare is 3A. though, i haven't any real way to verify their claims... it would be ncie to get some real measurements from some of these solar boards and the RAK 1W at it's most power hungry spikes

messages.author: Eddie
messages.text: I just received my kit and so far its been disappointing! I have it powered by USB with the same antenna as my other devices (other devices have batteries from their OEM). Bluetooth seems to drop just a few feet or even inches away. This happens on both of my Android phones (Pixel 9 Pro daily driver) and I plan on resurrecting a third phone that is in the box for ewaste recycling since that would be an older BLE standard. I can leave my other devices upstairs in the window, go downstairs below them and no BLE issues. Based on the absence of a message saying BLE has connected, it seems that it only sees a FRACTION of the packets that the ThinkNode M1 sees. But there's more... those missing packets in the RX log are not just from BLE dropping...the LORA side of the house is only seeing fraction of the packets since I see only a fraction of the messages in the public channel. My initial testing was on 1.13 and I upgraded my other devices from 1.12 to 1.13 to try to make it as scientific as possible. I downgraded to 1.12 (full erase) and the results are the same. I don't have any temp sensors, but its been cool to the touch so far. I am considering trying MeshTastic to see if it has the issues I have seen to see if it is bad hardware, but my experience with MT was so abysmal I hate that do that. 8-)

Is anyone getting stable production-ready results on the 3401? I had one working (or so I thought) at my house up in a tree. Took the box, mounted it on a frame, and took it to the site. I'm getting substantially worse performance from it than I was the 4631 test node I had at the site before. I basically swapped the two nodes. Took the 4631 home and put it back up in the same tree where the 1W was before. Now, I can't reach the downtown location which was no problem before the swapout. Put a 1W up in the tree at home, and STILL can't hit the downtown. Anyone have any thoughts? I'm about ready to call these crap and just go back to the 4631s.

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: So, I just got home and compiled a version of the v1.12 firmware (because it was working tollerably well for me) with the RX Boost config flag set to off, and installed it. I'm seeing a pretty consistent 5-10 db decrease in the noise floor compared to the standard v1.12 firmware (meaning, the custom is running -110 or so, vs -100 on the standard.). It swings between -113-> -108 vs -104->-100 or so.

messages.author: andyshinn
messages.text: i don't really know how it interacts with the noise floor and actual performance. i just know that it was a topic for previous 1W radios like the B&Q Station G2 where the automatic gain on the receive side could continue to creep up to a point where it eventually was almost completely deaf and there was benefit to setting the AGC reset to an interval that reset it periodically to keep it in check, which helped realy world performance

messages.author: TF-Mambo
messages.text: Got a response from RAK. Seems the Buzzer module has a LOW and HIGH setting that needs to be set low. I will keep looking around the FW to see what else can be fixed and corrected, ie. the constant Blue LED.

messages.reply_html:
ThatGuy
Back to the receive issue for a moment: As reported, I drove down and applied the agc.reset.interval CLI command to my problem-child repeater. Immediate signal improvement. On the test repeater at my house, I had applied a custom FW that had RX BOOST turned off. I also applied the AGC fix to it. Things were working well. To test, I flashed it back to standard v1.12 firmware. Performance dropped. I'm going to put it back to the custom firmware sometime this afternoon hopefully, to see if it returns to better signal. I think we may be making some progress on this issue.

messages.author: TF-Mambo
messages.text: Running into an interesting issue compiling custom for BLE. If I add -D RAK_BOARD to the [rak3401] build_flags and -D PIN_GPS_EN=-1 to the [env:RAK_3401_companion_radio_ble] build_flags, I get GPS enabled and working but has slow channel and contact loads and does not communicate with LoRa. Without these two build_flags I get no GPS but works fine. Thoughts?
messages.content_html: Running into an interesting issue compiling custom for BLE. If I add -D RAK_BOARD to the [rak3401] build_flags and -D PIN_GPS_EN=-1 to the [env:RAK_3401_companion_radio_ble] build_flags, I get GPS enabled and working but has slow channel and contact loads and does not communicate with LoRa. Without these two build_flags I get no GPS but works fine. Thoughts?

messages.author: andyshinn
messages.text: i believe -1 makes it go through a GPS auto-detection part for RAK. i would maybe first check that https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/rak13302/datasheet/#pin-definition doesn't step on any pins used by the GPS sensor. different sensor ports sometimes have different IO pin usages. also, the 19007 has different IO pins given to the sensor depending on the port so maybe confirming with https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/rak19007/datasheet/#connectors-for-wisblock-sensor if any of the IO pins are shared bby that sensor
messages.content_html: i believe -1 makes it go through a GPS auto-detection part for RAK. i would maybe first check that https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/rak13302/datasheet/#pin-definition doesn't step on any pins used by the GPS sensor. different sensor ports sometimes have different IO pin usages. also, the 19007 has different IO pins given to the sensor depending on the port so maybe confirming with https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/rak19007/datasheet/#connectors-for-wisblock-sensor if any of the IO pins are shared bby that sensor

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: So, I tried building a version of the 1.13 FW that had the toggle for RXBoost from that PR I shared recently. It worked on my RAK3401 at home, but when I installed it on my problem child repeater, it became unresponsive. After a couple of reinstalls, I fell back to one that just has RXBooster compiled off. It installed and the repeater came back up. Not sure what the issue was. Since install, we've noticed a significant increase in performance since turning RX Boost off. I think if you have a noisy environment, this is going to give significant improvements when using this RAK 3401/13302 board. I can't explain why it's working by citing specific stats like RSSI values etc, but it's connecting to things it wasn't before, and is passing traffic more reliably. (edited)

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: @ccw I'm not an expert, and there are several simultaneous issues going on here, so it can be kind of confusing. I'll explain my understanding of it, but there's a fair chance I don't fully understand. For a while, people have been observing that the RAK 1W boards, particularly in noisy RF environments, are "going deaf". Speculation on this has focus around a couple of different areas. 1) the automatic gain controller essentially dialing up the gain until the receiver overloads and can't hear anything through the noise, and then not properly resetting when the noise floor decreases. The AGC is supposed to adjust itself automatically. In order to force the board to reset it, people have been trying 'set agc.reset.interval ' to make the board reset itself every _ seconds (It has to be a multiple of 4). Some people have seen performance increases by doing so. I am one of them. It wasn't huge, but it was noticable. 2) There is also speculation that since the 13302 board has its own reception booster and filter on board, that the built in software RX Boost, which is turned on in the MC firmware by default it essentially causing a "double boost", and making the receiver go deaf. Some people have also seen performance increases by disabling the software RX Boost. Initially, you had to do this by compiling an custom firmware to turn it off, b/c the setting was housed in a compile time build flag. Somone created a PR that makes it user selectable in the repeater command line interface via the ' {get/set} radio.lna {on/off}' command. Then, yesterday, @weebl2000 discovered that the FW may not have had all of the necessary configuration for the 13302 board to be correctly powered and used, which may have been contributing to some of the issues. He patched the code to fix that. Some people also experience overheating issues with the 1W boards. Could be software problem. Might be defective hardware. (edited)

messages.reply_html:
ThatGuy
@ccw I'm not an expert, and there are several simultaneous issues going on here, so it can be kind of confusing. I'll explain my understanding of it, but there's a fair chance I don't fully understand. For a while, people have been observing that the RAK 1W boards, particularly in noisy RF environments, are "going deaf". Speculation on this has focus around a couple of different areas. 1) the automatic gain controller essentially dialing up the gain until the receiver overloads and can't hear anything through the noise, and then not properly resetting when the noise floor decreases. The AGC is supposed to adjust itself automatically. In order to force the board to reset it, people have been trying 'set agc.reset.interval ' to make the board reset itself every _ seconds (It has to be a multiple of 4). Some people have seen performance increases by doing so. I am one of them. It wasn't huge, but it was noticable. 2) There is also speculation that since the 13302 board has its own reception booster and filter on board, that the built in software RX Boost, which is turned on in the MC firmware by default it essentially causing a "double boost", and making the receiver go deaf. Some people have also seen performance increases by disabling the software RX Boost. Initially, you had to do this by compiling an custom firmware to turn it off, b/c the setting was housed in a compile time build flag. Somone created a PR that makes it user selectable in the repeater command line interface via the ' {get/set} radio.lna {on/off}' command. Then, yesterday, @weebl2000 discovered that the FW may not have had all of the necessary configuration for the 13302 board to be correctly powered and used, which may have been contributing to some of the issues. He patched the code to fix that. Some people also experience overheating issues with the 1W boards. Could be software problem. Might be defective hardware. (edited)

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: Hello all - new member here and new Meshcore user. I have one of these 1W RAK kits on order so I’m following along closely. I wanted to commend you for your determination to optimize this firmware. One quick question: what SX1262 power out level setting are you using? Reading the SKY66122-11 datasheet, a max input power level is +16dBm. I know the SX1262 can go up to +22dBm so I was curious how that aspect was being handled. The typical insertion loss of the SAW filter is about 2dB so I would think any SX1262 output power setting over 18dB might start over-driving the PA. Also, on the RX side for the SX1262, I don’t think the boosted gain setting is necessary seeing that the SKY66122-11 has 16dB of gain. Boosting in the SX1262 will just amplify the noise seeing the SKY66122-11 has just an OK noise figure. Anyway, not sure if this is helpful but wanted to say thanks again for what you are doing! (edited)

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: I tested mine as follows: Installed the v4 variant on my 3401 , rebooted. Logged into a remote repeater and sent an advert. Logged back into the modded 3401 repeater, and looked at the neighbors list with the radio.lna on. Recorded the observed SNR. Set radio.lna off, rebooted, and repeated the process. Results: Lna on: 5.75 db, 3.5 Lna off: 8.0 db, 8.5, 9.5. (edited)

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: I guess I have to question https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1836 CSD and CPS on the SKY66122 are both connected to WisBlock IO3 per https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/RAK13302/datasheet/ Setting IO3 to logic high would take it from shutdown/sleep to enabling the LNA on the SKY66122. Also, CTX is connected directly to the DIO2 pin on the SX1262, assuming that the SX1262 has been setup with “SetDIO2AsRfSwitchCtrl” in section 11.3 of the SX1262 datasheet. Similarly, the circuit is setup such that the SX1262 controls the TCXO via its DIO3 which would require “SetDIO3AsTcxoCtrl”. Finally, the boost regulator, U5, is controlled by WisBlock IO2 and only necessary if a battery is being used (jumper set to IN_5V) versus directly powering the RAK13302 via the 5V connection on the bottom and setting the jumper to EX_5V. My thoughts/theory is that maybe the control I/Os aren’t being properly handled per the circuit intention/implementation. If the CSD/CPS (IO3) is going low after TX for whatever reason, then the SKY66122 is effectively being turned off and that may explain the unit going “deaf”. (edited)
messages.content_html: I guess I have to question https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1836 CSD and CPS on the SKY66122 are both connected to WisBlock IO3 per https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/RAK13302/datasheet/ Setting IO3 to logic high would take it from shutdown/sleep to enabling the LNA on the SKY66122. Also, CTX is connected directly to the DIO2 pin on the SX1262, assuming that the SX1262 has been setup with “SetDIO2AsRfSwitchCtrl” in section 11.3 of the SX1262 datasheet. Similarly, the circuit is setup such that the SX1262 controls the TCXO via its DIO3 which would require “SetDIO3AsTcxoCtrl”. Finally, the boost regulator, U5, is controlled by WisBlock IO2 and only necessary if a battery is being used (jumper set to IN_5V) versus directly powering the RAK13302 via the 5V connection on the bottom and setting the jumper to EX_5V. My thoughts/theory is that maybe the control I/Os aren’t being properly handled per the circuit intention/implementation. If the CSD/CPS (IO3) is going low after TX for whatever reason, then the SKY66122 is effectively being turned off and that may explain the unit going “deaf”. (edited)

messages.text: All of my comments above rely on https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/RAK13302/datasheet/ being accurate but I am seeing discrepancies between the schematics and the connector diagram. For example, EX_5V (via Schottky D1) should not be connected to USB+ (Pin 7) on the board-to-board connector and have it power the RAK3401 properly - it should be connected to VBUS (Pin 9). The whole documentation is a bit of a mess from RAK…

The whole documentation is a bit of a mess from RAK…

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: I am concerned that, if the EX_5V is wired up to USB+ like it shows on the schematics, it could cause some issues with the RAK3401 module and may be a cause of the overheating. The device could be getting back-powered through ESD diodes or some other path that’s not intentional (should be VBUS). If overheating happens when using EX_5V but not when using a battery+USB input (IN_5V jumper setting), that could explain things. I have a support question out to RAK questioning their schematics and if it is indeed how the RAK13302 module is wired. (edited)

messages.text: Duplicate of what I posted on GitHub below. I'm liking what I'm seeing. This is #1836 - Variant 4 AGC reset set at 16. Updated Astor Repeater and tested rak3401-agc-and-lna-remote-registerpatch. It's hearing very well vs a comparable observer in capturing all packets, indicating that it is not going deaf for extended seconds at a time. I've got a hill node LOS that I put up on 1s delay in case I miss a packet while tx at this observer. Noise floor has been stable at about -102 with noise spikes correlating with high rx utilization in the graphs. I'll keep this up for a few days at least and update if any issues arise. (edited)

messages.author: Fokai
messages.text: I was asked to take a look at LNA on vs LNA off in the CLI. Both scenarios stayed up for ~12hrs and I checked the snapshop in the picture many times and it was very consistent with the LNA on performing slightly better than LNA off. Splitting hairs with ~1% improvement in packets heard for LNA ON, but it was consistently better. Again, this performance was for my particular station (Astor) environment against a closely performing monitor a few miles away that has very good PDX coverage. Overall, the PR is working very well. Nice work! This was all done with interval reset at 0. I never reset the AGC at any point during these tests. It's actually working well IMO (edited)
messages.content_html: I was asked to take a look at LNA on vs LNA off in the CLI. Both scenarios stayed up for ~12hrs and I checked the snapshop in the picture many times and it was very consistent with the LNA on performing slightly better than LNA off. Splitting hairs with ~1% improvement in packets heard for LNA ON, but it was consistently better. Again, this performance was for my particular station (Astor) environment against a closely performing monitor a few miles away that has very good PDX coverage. Overall, the PR is working very well. Nice work! This was all done with interval reset at 0. I never reset the AGC at any point during these tests. It's actually working well IMO (edited)

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: So, in case anyone is interested, after I drove off last night, I discovered that the replacement board was not working as intended. Couldn't hit it from more than a 3-4 blocks away. Re-flashed the one I took off last night with weebl2000's latest above , and took it back down there at lunch today, reinstalled after some troubleshooting, and it's working marvelously. I'm getting direct connect reception where I've never have before. I'm thinking I've got a bad transmitter section on the new one I tried to put up last night. It was hearing OK, but it wouldn't respond and nothing could hear it outside of its immediate area. Going to have to do some testing on it. QC seems to be an issue on these 1W boards.
messages.content_html: So, in case anyone is interested, after I drove off last night, I discovered that the replacement board was not working as intended. Couldn't hit it from more than a 3-4 blocks away. Re-flashed the one I took off last night with weebl2000's latest above , and took it back down there at lunch today, reinstalled after some troubleshooting, and it's working marvelously. I'm getting direct connect reception where I've never have before. I'm thinking I've got a bad transmitter section on the new one I tried to put up last night. It was hearing OK, but it wouldn't respond and nothing could hear it outside of its immediate area. Going to have to do some testing on it. QC seems to be an issue on these 1W boards.

messages.author: thetooth
messages.text: also reporting another success with this fw. i am about to put up a repeater and did some testing of the solar charging before going on the roof, stock fw i was only getting 1.5 days from a 2400mAh battery with sun hitting the panel so figured the charging circuit was bad but checking the current it was pulling 50mA continuously which is obviously not normal. this firmware I am seeing basically nothing and still around 50% state of charge after 3 days without solar connected

messages.reply_html:
ccw
If you want to measure the attenuation in the filter you could just do it with a function generator and a scope. Just heads up don’t make your comparison with the 1W rack or a V4. The radios with an amplifier on the front end will just change their gain settings to achieve a target noise floor, so you won’t get anything meaningful from the numbers reported in the app

messages.author: ccw
messages.text: Like you, I haven’t checked exhaustively, but as best I can tell, not bad. There’s the issue I was mentioning, namely that it’ll adjust the amplifier gain to keep the noise floor around -105 pretty much independent of what’s going on around you. If I drive up to a mystery transmitter that takes my V3 up to like -80 or worse and hold it there, the rak will get a momentary spike and go back to baseline and then a negative spike when I drive away, so there’s nothing to compare to directly without more effort, but my sense is that of the radios I have, the rak hears best

There’s the issue I was mentioning, namely that it’ll adjust the amplifier gain to keep the noise floor around -105 pretty much independent of what’s going on around you. messages.author: ccw
messages.text: For an authoritative answer you want either an expert on these radios or experiment, but in principle, sure: among the downsides filters aren’t perfect, so every layer you add is throwing away signal too and costing transmit performance. My guess is if you try the built in filter and the background noise is still bad enough to cause you problems, you’d want to try a different strategy than just stacking up more filters of similar performance.
messages.content_html: For an authoritative answer you want either an expert on these radios or experiment, but in principle, sure: among the downsides filters aren’t perfect, so every layer you add is throwing away signal too and costing transmit performance. My guess is if you try the built in filter and the background noise is still bad enough to cause you problems, you’d want to try a different strategy than just stacking up more filters of similar performance.

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: Since we have a quality group of testers in this thread, could I get y'all's help on testing this PR? It's to put a companion into OTA mode remotely for OTA updates, so you could use the Nordic app (other others) to update the firmware over BLE, without having to physically hit the button or plug up a cable. I think I have it working. I've tested on the RAK 4631 and the 3401, but I'd like to make sure the results are reproducable. Ultimately, there will be a button for it in the MC app. Until then, there is a python script that will trigger it. If you get a chance, try it out and let me know if it works for you. https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/2078 (edited)
messages.content_html: Since we have a quality group of testers in this thread, could I get y'all's help on testing this PR? It's to put a companion into OTA mode remotely for OTA updates, so you could use the Nordic app (other others) to update the firmware over BLE, without having to physically hit the button or plug up a cable. I think I have it working. I've tested on the RAK 4631 and the 3401, but I'd like to make sure the results are reproducable. Ultimately, there will be a button for it in the MC app. Until then, there is a python script that will trigger it. If you get a chance, try it out and let me know if it works for you. https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/2078 (edited)

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: I'm not convinced that we have the transmit side of things working quite right on the 3401 boards. Is anyone else experiencing less than expected transmit performance? For example, we put a node up this weekend with 3401, powered by a solar panel that has 26000 mAh of battery, with a 12v to 5v converter that puts out up to 10A of power. Running to the 5v_EXT port with jumper set accordingly. So, power shouldn't be an issue. It's running a Diamond BC920 antenna. It's mouted at 190 feet up a tower. There was a 4631-based node about 90 feet lower on the tower, with a similar (if slightly inferior) antenna. I adverted from both and then looked at my next highest node that can hear that area. The 4631 advert was received at +4.25 dB. The 3401 was -4.50dB . Repeated and got the same results. Reception on the 3401 is outstanding however. Connect quality to the new node is a lot less reliable than the 4631-based node was. Anyone have and thoughts/guesses? (edited)
messages.content_html: I'm not convinced that we have the transmit side of things working quite right on the 3401 boards. Is anyone else experiencing less than expected transmit performance? For example, we put a node up this weekend with 3401, powered by a solar panel that has 26000 mAh of battery, with a 12v to 5v converter that puts out up to 10A of power. Running to the 5v_EXT port with jumper set accordingly. So, power shouldn't be an issue. It's running a Diamond BC920 antenna. It's mouted at 190 feet up a tower. There was a 4631-based node about 90 feet lower on the tower, with a similar (if slightly inferior) antenna. I adverted from both and then looked at my next highest node that can hear that area. The 4631 advert was received at +4.25 dB. The 3401 was -4.50dB . Repeated and got the same results. Reception on the 3401 is outstanding however. Connect quality to the new node is a lot less reliable than the 4631-based node was. Anyone have and thoughts/guesses? (edited)

messages.author: Deverox
messages.text: Interesting. I have 2 of these boards. One as a repeater in my window and one as my car node. They are replacing 2 HELTEC v4. I haven't had a good way to test them but ever since getting them set up I have been kind of disappointed with them. The first few days I felt like I wasn't getting as many messages out as I was with the HELTECs the all of the sudden they were doing great, then I would have a few days of lackluster performance. I do notice one of the repeaters near me some days I have an okish signal then some days it's -7.3. it's the reason I haven't finished building out the solar boxes and put them up on my roof. I keep hoping it just needs a few more firmware updates to get there but I'm losing hope. I'm thinking of getting a few 4631 boards and using those.

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: How does the Meshcore TX power setting compare to the actual SX1262 TX output power as an input to the SKY66122 amp?
messages.content_html: How does the Meshcore TX power setting compare to the actual SX1262 TX output power as an input to the SKY66122 amp?
messages.reply_html:
Aenigma
Thanks! Maybe RAK handles this in the 13302 module such that a setting of 22 will safely achieve the max output with the PA. I just never like pushing things to the limit and I was wondering if some of the overheating concerns were related to TX power settings, how the power is supplied to the kit (external vs internal), or something else (I saw some mentions of a buzzer circuit). (edited)

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: I looked more closely at the schematics - there is a Pi attenuator between the SX1262 and the SKY66122 of about -20.6dB. When you add in the insertion loss of the SAW filter, setting the SX1262 to +22dBm puts about 0dBm into the SKY66122. Since the S21 (small signal gain) is about 33dB, that should get you up to the max +30dBm (1W) at the output at 5V. (edited)
messages.content_html: I looked more closely at the schematics - there is a Pi attenuator between the SX1262 and the SKY66122 of about -20.6dB. When you add in the insertion loss of the SAW filter, setting the SX1262 to +22dBm puts about 0dBm into the SKY66122. Since the S21 (small signal gain) is about 33dB, that should get you up to the max +30dBm (1W) at the output at 5V. (edited)

messages.author: weebl2000
messages.text: Yeah meshcore just tells the SX1262 how much power to output
messages.content_html: Yeah meshcore just tells the SX1262 how much power to output

messages.author: ccw
messages.text: So just to toss out a possibility: might be two problems here, one a consequence of the other. I’ve had one of these boards behaving very well for weeks on weebl’s variant 4 way up the thread, no overheating, apparently infinite battery life. Left it on the porch the other day and it got hot out there, like 130F air temp and idk how hot the board got. It locked up and was still >140 when it became responsive again. Lost a bunch of the nonvolatile memory contents, or at least its record of what was stored where. Anywho, point being: if there’s a bug overheating the MCU, lockups and other misbehavior may occur due to the heat itself

messages.author: Deverox
messages.text: So yeah it was a power issue... So on my broken one I noticed when I just had it plugged in with USB, in the meshcore app it said it was 0% battery. I figured that was normal since I thought USB didn't deliver enough power. It was just there for data and to charge if you had battery hooked up to it. Well I accidently hit the 0% and it showed me the voltage... It said 1.5v. I hooked up a new one with just USB attached in the app it always said 100% and 4.20v.. I must have shorted something and killed something on the board. It wasn't getting enough power and wasn't charging the battery either. I bet that's why I wasn't getting message out that far too.. I bet it wasn't transmitting anywhere near 1w.

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: Per the RAK information, the battery must be attached if you are using the USB port and have the jumper set to internal 5 V. The battery gets boosted to 5 V on the radio module, and the USB alone will not power the radio module.
messages.content_html: Per the RAK information, the battery must be attached if you are using the USB port and have the jumper set to internal 5 V. The battery gets boosted to 5 V on the radio module, and the USB alone will not power the radio module.

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: For your broken board, does the red charge light ever come on? Or was that the one where it would only be a dim red light? I suspect the charger IC is the problem. It’s not properly sourcing power from USB and delivering it to the battery terminals. That would explain why it would show 0% versus the one that does work showing 100% when no battery is attached. (edited)
messages.content_html: For your broken board, does the red charge light ever come on? Or was that the one where it would only be a dim red light? I suspect the charger IC is the problem. It’s not properly sourcing power from USB and delivering it to the battery terminals. That would explain why it would show 0% versus the one that does work showing 100% when no battery is attached. (edited)

messages.author: andyshinn
messages.text: the problem is you need USB that can safely do >1.5A and sustain the 5V. if you are powering from your vehicle 12V supply then you want to get a USB charger that can do closer to 2A output. then you can cut one end of the USB cable and go into the EX_5V connector cable to do USB 5V -> EX_5V (set the jumper to EX_5V). you don't need anything else connected if you do EX_5V only
messages.content_html: the problem is you need USB that can safely do >1.5A and sustain the 5V. if you are powering from your vehicle 12V supply then you want to get a USB charger that can do closer to 2A output. then you can cut one end of the USB cable and go into the EX_5V connector cable to do USB 5V -> EX_5V (set the jumper to EX_5V). you don't need anything else connected if you do EX_5V only

messages.author: Not Sure
messages.text: The "22" max setting we see in software controls the SX1262 chip. A separate (but integrated in the package) amplifier bumps it to 30dBm in the 1W. So there shouldn't be any way overdrive it by software unless the 1262 has a fault. The same applies to 22 being 28dBm out on the Heltec v4. Think of ~22 as a scaling factor not a set number as it was before higher power chips came along.
messages.content_html: The "22" max setting we see in software controls the SX1262 chip. A separate (but integrated in the package) amplifier bumps it to 30dBm in the 1W. So there shouldn't be any way overdrive it by software unless the 1262 has a fault. The same applies to 22 being 28dBm out on the Heltec v4. Think of ~22 as a scaling factor not a set number as it was before higher power chips came along.

messages.author: 👽 wazmac
messages.text: I have just been testing with a local contact in Sydney and we have some interesting observations. We have clear LoS over 31km and a pair of RAK 1W repeaters. Between these two nodes we are getting consistent birdirectional SNR of 10.5db to 11db with relatively high noise floors of 84 and 94. The interesting thing though is that they do not seem not outstanding at all when communicating with other nodes. (edited)
messages.content_html: I have just been testing with a local contact in Sydney and we have some interesting observations. We have clear LoS over 31km and a pair of RAK 1W repeaters. Between these two nodes we are getting consistent birdirectional SNR of 10.5db to 11db with relatively high noise floors of 84 and 94. The interesting thing though is that they do not seem not outstanding at all when communicating with other nodes. (edited)

messages.author: Lbibass
messages.text: I’m not shocked. Those noise floors aren’t great. Especially the 84 db one.

messages.author: bp-gone
messages.text: oh, by the way, in case you are interested. Today I tested out using supercapacitors (and a hybrid capacitor) on the battery connector to satisfy the "USB (or solar) plus battery" requirement for the INT setting. I use external solar charge controllers like the DFR0559 but it only does 1A and can't drive the RAK by itself. Put a supercap on the battery connector and it solves the problem.
messages.content_html: oh, by the way, in case you are interested. I use external solar charge controllers like the DFR0559 but it only does 1A and can't drive the RAK by itself. Put a supercap on the battery connector and it solves the problem.

messages.author: siliex
messages.text: fwiw, aside from accidentally burning out the radio (which was user error, not the board's fault) I've only had good experiences with this board so far - just got my replacement today and put it in, working great. Using the internal power option with 4x 18650s and solar, no additional charge controller (yet? not sure if I'm going to get one). No additional modules on the board though I have an RTC on the way. Both boards I've run have had way better performance than the 4631 I was using in the exact same spot, though they also had a higher noise floor which is I guess expected. Weird how variable people's experiences are with this thing.

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: For what it is worth, I was finally able to lay my hands on a TinySA to test one of my RAK 1W kits that wasn't working as expected. As I suspected, it's a burned out transmitter section. Tried it on multiple versions of the firmware, and in all possible power configs. Maximum transmit I could get out of it was -35 db. I compared it with a new-in-package 1W, which showed +29.5 db or so using the same testing setup and powered solely through the EXT 5V connector with appropriate jumper selection. While I was at it, I went ahead and tested the new working board with the jumper set to INT 5V, and using only battery and then only USB, and it had underwhelming transmit performance on both...in the range of -10db. Powering with both Battery and USB transmitted at +29.4 db or so, as designed. So, they aren't kidding about needing to use both sources simultaneously if you're powering from the main board and not using the EXT_5V connector. If you decide to replicate this testing yourself, please note that you **__need ** a 40db or so signal attenuator to avoid burning out the front end on the TinySA, which is rated apparently up to about 10db. 1W = roughly 30db, so the 40 attenuator gives you plenty of room. You just have to do the math at the end. (edited)

messages.text: TinySA setup: I used a TinySA Ultra+. Calibrate the unit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. After this, hook up as shown below. Tap the Screen. Hit Frequency. I set up the frequency range on the device to scan from 910.40 to 911.60, since I use 910.525 as my center frequency. This keeps the scanning path short and makes it less likely you’ll miss the transmission peak. Back out to the main menu, then Tap “Trace”. If it isn’t already selected, pick a trace you want to use. I used Trace 1. Click “Enable”. Then click “Calc Off”. Pick “Max Hold”. This will cause it to lock the highest peak on the screen so you can read it. After you’ve taken a measurement, and want to do another, tap the screen again, and hit “Max Hold” again. This will reset the pointer peak. You can do this over and over as you take measurements.

messages.author: beala
messages.text: Wait so powering from a battery only (connected to the base board with INT_5V) also resulted in less output? That's surprising to me! This was a fully charged 18650 li-ion?
messages.content_html: Wait so powering from a battery only (connected to the base board with INT_5V) also resulted in less output? That's surprising to me! This was a fully charged 18650 li-ion?
messages.reply_html:
ThatGuy
For what it is worth, I was finally able to lay my hands on a TinySA to test one of my RAK 1W kits that wasn't working as expected. As I suspected, it's a burned out transmitter section. Tried it on multiple versions of the firmware, and in all possible power configs. Maximum transmit I could get out of it was -35 db. I compared it with a new-in-package 1W, which showed +29.5 db or so using the same testing setup and powered solely through the EXT5V connector with appropriate jumper selection. While I was at it, I went ahead and tested the new working board with the jumper set to INT5V, and using only battery and then only USB, and it had underwhelming transmit performance on both...in the range of -10db. Powering with both Battery and USB transmitted at +29.4 db or so, as designed. So, they aren't kidding about needing to use both sources simultaneously if you're powering from the main board and not using the EXT_5V connector. If you decide to replicate this testing yourself, please note that you **__need** a 40db or so signal attenuator to avoid burning out the front end on the TinySA, which is rated apparently up to about 10db. 1W = roughly 30db, so the 40 attenuator gives you plenty of room. You just have to do the math at the end. (edited)

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: Yes, at least as far as I can tell. It seems RAK has changed the wording on the docs yet again , but previously, it said you had to do either 5V EXT for everything, or Battery + USB or Battery + a 5v source into the Solar Port. I don't understand why 5v into the USB port wouldn't do it alone, but earlier versions of the docs said it wouldn't, and my testing supports that. I plugged up USB, forgot the battery for a moment, and switched it over to INT, and was getting lower than expected transmit. Then, I was like, 'oh yeah, the battery', plugged it in, and it jumped up to 30db immediately. I don't have the known good board here anymore. I sent it out for deployment today, so I can't reverify immediately. Once I lay hands back on a working 1W, I'll verify this result again.

messages.author: Codes
messages.text: For what it's worth, I am running solar 2 repeaters and my pocket node on the internal 5v regulator and a honkin' battery. We did a TX power test this weekend with a fully charged 3000mah pouch cell and showed 1.2W, as expected. didn't matter if we had USB plugged in or not for the battery nearly fully charged. We didn't try other battery charge states, or smaller batteries. All my nodes are doing really well in terms of power output as far as I can tell, but they all also have huge batteries. 3000mah for my pocket node, 8000mah for one repeater, and at least 15,000mah for the other. (edited)

messages.author: Aenigma
messages.text: For those wondering about the power paths, if you look at the schematics, the SKY66122 FEM only gets powered from either the EX_5V input OR the 5V boost converter which takes the battery voltage as its input. The USB VBUS is NOT connected to the amp, that’s why it won’t work properly with just USB input. (edited)
messages.content_html: For those wondering about the power paths, if you look at the schematics, the SKY66122 FEM only gets powered from either the EX_5V input OR the 5V boost converter which takes the battery voltage as its input. The USB VBUS is NOT connected to the amp, that’s why it won’t work properly with just USB input. (edited)

messages.text: Seems more in line with what I was seeing on the 4631. Packets received is 1423, packet errors is 1001. I think total packets received does not include errors? So total seen would be 2424 (rx + errors). Not entirely sure how the stat works. Checked the code, my assumption is correct. When I checked it before adding the filter it was around 14k rx and 14k errors from a few days of uptime. Will keep the thread updated over the next few days in case anything changes. (edited)

messages.author: Deverox
messages.text: The replacement RAK 3401 got here today... Installed it and it was working fine for all of 10 minutes. Flashed the newest meshcore Repeater firmware and in the middle of setting it up it locked up on me. Had to reboot it. It locked back up a few minutes later. Never could finish getting it set up. Noticed it was very warm to the touch. Reflashed the test firmware they had me use during testing with tech support... It's showing 2v again... I think this board freid the new module... Sending them an email back...
